label: ai
topic: What the final Paris Agreement actually contains
text:
The Paris Agreement adopted on 12 December 2015 runs to twenty-nine articles and is built around three interlocking pillars. The first is mitigation: every party must prepare, communicate, and maintain a nationally determined contribution setting out what it intends to do to limit emissions, and must submit a new contribution every five years that represents a progression beyond the previous one. The second pillar is adaptation, acknowledging that some degree of warming is now unavoidable and asking countries to plan for its consequences and to share information on what is working. The third pillar is finance and support: developed countries are expected to provide financial resources to help developing nations both cut emissions and cope with impacts.

Underpinning all three pillars is an enhanced transparency framework requiring countries to report on their emissions and on the steps they are taking, with a global stocktake every five years to assess collective progress against the long-term temperature goal. The transparency provisions were among the most heavily negotiated elements of the agreement, because they determine how much scrutiny each country's claims will actually receive. Developing countries pushed for flexibility in reporting requirements, arguing that many lacked the technical capacity to produce the detailed inventories that developed nations compile annually. The final text includes built-in flexibility for countries that need it while setting a common framework that all parties are expected to converge toward over time.

One of the most commented-on features of the agreement is what it deliberately omits: the nationally determined contributions themselves are not legally binding. The obligation to submit and update them is binding; the targets within them are not. This architecture was chosen specifically to allow the United States to join without seeking Senate ratification, which would have been impossible given the political climate in Washington. The distinction between binding process and non-binding substance became a defining characteristic of the Paris model and the source of much of the subsequent debate about its effectiveness.

The agreement also addresses loss and damage, a concept that recognises the harm climate change inflicts on vulnerable nations that goes beyond what adaptation can address. This was a hard-won inclusion for small island states and least developed countries, though the final text explicitly excludes liability or compensation claims, a condition the United States and other developed nations insisted upon. The resulting provision acknowledged the problem without creating a legal pathway for redress, leaving the question of who pays for irreversible climate impacts unresolved but formally on the international agenda for the first time.
